items.q: What is the difference between the motor effect and electromagnetic induction?
items.correct: The motor effect produces a force on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field; induction produces an emf when flux linkage changes.
items.misconception: They are the same phenomenon described in different words.
items.random_wrong: The motor effect only works on DC; induction only works on AC.
items.why: Students see both involve coils, magnets and electricity and don't separate 'current causes force' (motor) from 'changing flux causes emf' (induction).

items.q: What is the difference between mass and weight?
items.correct: Mass is the amount of matter (kg); weight is the gravitational force on that mass (N) and depends on g.
items.misconception: Mass and weight are the same thing, just expressed in different units.
items.random_wrong: Mass changes with location but weight does not.
items.why: Students confuse mass with weight because everyday English uses 'weight' for both, and bathroom scales display mass in kg labelled as 'weight'.

items.q: What does specific latent heat measure?
items.correct: The energy required to change the state of 1 kg of a substance without changing its temperature.
items.misconception: The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 kg by 1 degree.
items.random_wrong: The temperature at which a substance melts or boils.
items.why: Students confuse specific latent heat with specific heat capacity because both involve 'heat' and 'kg', missing the no-temperature-change aspect of latent heat.

items.q: Why do drawing pins have sharp points?
items.why: Correct answer: To create high pressure. “To look pretty” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Pascals. “Newtons” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A block has mass 60 g and volume 20 cm³. What is its density in g/cm³?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
